CodeNomad gives you the cockpit. 8: 9: ![Multi-instance workspace](docs/screenshots/newSession.png) 10: 11: --- 12: 13: ## Features 14: 15: - **🚀 Multi-Instance Workspace** 16: - **🌐 Remote Access** 17: - **🧠 Session Management** 18: - **🎙️ Voice Input & Speech** 19: - **🌳 Git Worktrees** 20: - **💬 Rich Message Experience** 21: - **🧩 SideCars** 22: - **⌨️ Command Palette** 23: - **📁 File System Browser** 24: - **🔐 Authentication & Security** 25: - **🔔 Notifications** 26: - **🎨 Theming** 27: - **🌍 Internationalization** 28: 29: --- 30: 31: ## Getting Started 32: 33: ### 🖥️ Desktop App 34: 35: Available as both Electron and Tauri builds — choose based on your preference. 36: 37: Download the latest installer for your platform from [Releases](https://github.com/shantur/CodeNomad/releases). 38: 39: | Platform | Formats | 40: |----------|---------| 41: | macOS | DMG, ZIP (Universal: Intel + Apple Silicon) | 42: | Windows | NSIS Installer, ZIP (x64, ARM64) | 43: | Linux | AppImage, deb, tar.gz (x64, ARM64) | 44: 45: ### 💻 CodeNomad Server 46: 47: Run as a local server and access via browser. Perfect for remote development. 48: 49: ```bash 50: npx @neuralnomads/codenomad --launch 51: ``` 52: 53: See [Server Documentation](packages/server/README.md) for flags, TLS, auth, and remote access. 54: 55: ### 🧪 Dev Releases 56: 57: Bleeding-edge builds from the `dev` branch: 58: 59: ```bash 60: npx @neuralnomads/codenomad-dev --launch 61: ``` 62: 63: --- 64: 65: ## SideCars 66: 67: SideCars let you open local web tools inside CodeNomad as tabs. 68: 69:
70: Configuration 71: 72: - **Name**: Display name used in CodeNomad 73: - **Port**: Local HTTP or HTTPS service running on `127.0.0.1:` 74: - **Base path**: Mounted under `/sidecars/:id` 75: - **Prefix mode**: 76: - **Preserve prefix** forwards the full `/sidecars/:id/...` path upstream 77: - **Strip prefix** removes `/sidecars/:id` before forwarding the request upstream 78: 79:
80: 81:
82: VSCode (OpenVSCode Server) 83: 84: Run with Docker: 85: 86: ```bash 87: docker run -it --init -p 8000:3000 -v "${HOME}:${HOME}:cached" -e HOME=${HOME} gitpod/openvscode-server --server-base-path /sidecars/vscode 88: ``` 89: 90: Add SideCar as: 91: 92: - **Name**: `VSCode` 93: - **Port**: `http://127.0.0.1:8000` 94: - **Base path**: `/sidecars/vscode` 95: - **Prefix mode**: `Preserve prefix` 96: 97:
98: 99:
100: Terminal (ttyd) 101: 102: Run with: 103: 104: ```bash 105: ttyd --writable zsh 106: ``` 107: 108: Add SideCar as: 109: 110: - **Name**: `Terminal` 111: - **Port**: `http://127.0.0.1:7681` 112: - **Base path**: `/sidecars/terminal` 113: - **Prefix mode**: `Strip prefix` 114: 115:
116: 117: --- 118: 119: ## Requirements 120: 121: - **[OpenCode CLI](https://opencode.ai)** — must be installed and in your `PATH` 122: - **Node.js 18+** — for server mode or building from source 123: 124: --- 125: 126: ## Development 127: 128: CodeNomad is a monorepo built with: 129: 130: | Package | Description | 131: |---------|-------------| 132: | **[packages/server](packages/server/README.md)** | Core logic & CLI — workspaces, OpenCode proxy, API, auth, speech | 133: | **[packages/ui](packages/ui/README.md)** | SolidJS frontend — reactive, fast, beautiful | 134: | **[packages/electron-app](packages/electron-app/README.md)** | Desktop shell — process management, IPC, native dialogs | 135: | **[packages/tauri-app](packages/tauri-app)** | Tauri desktop shell (experimental) | 136: 137: ### Quick Start 138: 139: ```bash 140: git clone https://github.com/NeuralNomadsAI/CodeNomad.git 141: cd CodeNomad 142: npm install 143: npm run dev 144: ``` 145: 146: --- 147: 148: ## Troubleshooting 149: 150:
151: macOS: "CodeNomad.app is damaged and can't be opened" 152: 153: Gatekeeper flag due to missing notarization. Clear the quarantine attribute: 154: 155: ```bash 156: xattr -dr com.apple.quarantine /Applications/CodeNomad.app 157: ``` 158: 159: On Intel Macs, also check **System Settings → Privacy & Security** on first launch. 160:
161: 162:
163: Linux (Wayland + NVIDIA): Tauri App closes immediately 164: 165: WebKitGTK DMA-BUF/GBM issue. Run with: 166: 167: ```bash 168: WEBKIT_DISABLE_DMABUF_RENDERER=1 codenomad 169: ``` 170: 171: See full workaround in the original README. 172:
173: 174: --- 175: 176: ## Community 177: 178: [![Star History](https://api.star-history.com/svg?repos=NeuralNomadsAI/CodeNomad&type=Date)](https://star-history.com/#NeuralNomadsAI/CodeNomad&Date) 179: 180: --- 181: 182: **Built with ♥ by [Neural Nomads](https://github.com/NeuralNomadsAI)** · [MIT License](LICENSE) (End of file - total 182 lines) Instructions from: /home/user-x/Projects/CodeNomad/AGENTS.md # AGENT NOTES ## Styling Guidelines - Reuse the existing token & utility layers before introducing new CSS variables or custom properties. Extend `src/styles/tokens.css` / `src/styles/utilities.css` if a shared pattern is needed. - Keep aggregate entry files (e.g., `src/styles/controls.css`, `messaging.css`, `panels.css`) lean—they should only `@import` feature-specific subfiles located inside `src/styles/{components|messaging|panels}`. - When adding new component styles, place them beside their peers in the scoped subdirectory (e.g., `src/styles/messaging/new-part.css`) and import them from the corresponding aggregator file. - Prefer smaller, focused style files (≈150 lines or less) over large monoliths. Split by component or feature area if a file grows beyond that size. - Co-locate reusable UI patterns (buttons, selectors, dropdowns, etc.) under `src/styles/components/` and avoid redefining the same utility classes elsewhere. - Document any new styling conventions or directory additions in this file so future changes remain consistent. ## Coding Principles - Favor KISS by keeping modules narrowly scoped and limiting public APIs to what callers actually need. - Uphold DRY: share helpers via dedicated modules before copy/pasting logic across stores, components, or scripts. - Enforce single responsibility; split large files when concerns diverge (state, actions, API, events, etc.). - Prefer composable primitives (signals, hooks, utilities) over deep inheritance or implicit global state. - When adding platform integrations (SSE, IPC, SDK), isolate them in thin adapters that surface typed events/actions. ## Multi-Language Support (i18n) The UI uses a small custom i18n layer (no ICU/messageformat). When building features, never hardcode user-visible strings. - **Runtime API:** use `useI18n()` in components (`const { t } = useI18n();`) and `tGlobal(...)` in stores/non-component code. - Implementation: `packages/ui/src/lib/i18n/index.tsx` - **Where messages live:** `packages/ui/src/lib/i18n/messages//` as TypeScript objects (`"flat.dot.keys": "string"`). - Each locale has an `index.ts` that merges message parts; duplicate keys throw at build time. - Merge helper: `packages/ui/src/lib/i18n/messages/merge.ts` - **Adding a new string:** add it to the appropriate `.../messages/en/*.ts` part file, then add the same key to each other locale’s corresponding file. - Missing translations fall back to English (and finally to the key), so gaps can be easy to miss. - **Interpolation:** placeholders are simple `{name}` replacements (word characters only). Avoid placeholders like `{file-name}`. - **Pluralization:** handle manually via separate keys like `something.one` / `something.other` and choose in code. - **Adding a new language:** add a new `messages//` folder + `index.ts`, register it in `packages/ui/src/lib/i18n/index.tsx`, and add it to the language picker in `packages/ui/src/components/folder-selection-view.tsx`. - **Locale persistence:** the selected locale is stored in app preferences (`locale`) and persisted via the server config (default `~/.config/codenomad/config.json`). - **Avoid English-only paths:** do not import `enMessages` directly in feature code; always go through `t(...)` so locale changes apply. ## File Length Guidelines (Highlight Only) We track file size as a refactoring signal. When you touch or create files, highlight oversized files so the team can plan refactors when time permits. - Source files: warn after ~500 lines; target limit ~800 lines - Test files: highlight after ~1000 lines Behavior for agents: - Do not refactor solely to satisfy these thresholds. - When a change touches a file that exceeds the warning/limit, mention it in your final response and include the file path and approximate line count. - When creating new files, aim to stay under the thresholds unless there's a clear reason. ## Tooling Preferences - Use the `edit` tool for modifying existing files; prefer it over other editing methods. - Use the `write` tool only when creating new files from scratch.

<content>
1: # Building CodeNomad Binaries
2: 
3: This guide explains how to build distributable binaries for CodeNomad.
4: 
5: ## Prerequisites
6: 
7: - **Bun** - Package manager and runtime
8: - **Node.js** - For electron-builder
9: - **Electron Builder** - Installed via devDependencies
10: 
11: ## Quick Start
12: 
13: All commands now run inside the workspace packages. From the repo root you can target the Electron app package directly:
14: 
15: ```bash
16: npm run build --workspace @neuralnomads/codenomad-electron-app
17: ```
18: 
19: ### Build for Current Platform (macOS default)
20: 
21: ```bash
22: bun run build:binaries
23: ```
24: 
25: This builds for macOS (Universal - Intel + Apple Silicon) by default.
26: 
27: ## Platform-Specific Builds
28: 
29: ### macOS
30: 
31: ```bash
32: # Universal (Intel + Apple Silicon) - Recommended
33: bun run build:mac
34: 
35: # Intel only (x64)
36: bun run build:mac-x64
37: 
38: # Apple Silicon only (ARM64)
39: bun run build:mac-arm64
40: ```
41: 
42: **Output formats:** `.dmg`, `.zip`
43: 
44: ### Windows
45: 
46: ```bash
47: # x64 (64-bit Intel/AMD)
48: bun run build:win
49: 
50: # ARM64 (Windows on ARM)
51: bun run build:win-arm64
52: ```
53: 
54: **Output formats:** `.exe` (NSIS installer), `.zip`
55: 
56: ### Linux
57: 
58: ```bash
59: # x64 (64-bit)
60: bun run build:linux
61: 
62: # ARM64
63: bun run build:linux-arm64
64: ```
65: 
66: **Output formats:** `.AppImage`, `.deb`, `.tar.gz`
67: 
68: ### Build All Platforms
69: 
70: ```bash
71: bun run build:all
72: ```
73: 
74: ⚠️ **Note:** Cross-platform builds may have limitations. Build on the target platform for best results.
75: 
76: ## Build Process
77: 
78: The build script performs these steps:
79: 
80: 1. **Build @neuralnomads/codenomad** → Produces the CLI `dist/` bundle (also rebuilds the UI assets it serves)
81: 2. **Compile TypeScript + bundle with Vite** → Electron main, preload, and renderer output in `dist/`
82: 3. **Package with electron-builder** → Platform-specific binaries
83: 
84: ## Output
85: 
86: Binaries are generated in the `release/` directory:
87: 
88: ```
89: release/
90: ├── CodeNomad-0.1.0-mac-universal.dmg
91: ├── CodeNomad-0.1.0-mac-universal.zip
92: ├── CodeNomad-0.1.0-win-x64.exe
93: ├── CodeNomad-0.1.0-linux-x64.AppImage
94: └── ...
95: ```
96: 
97: ## File Naming Convention
98: 
99: ```
100: CodeNomad-{version}-{os}-{arch}.{ext}
101: ```
102: 
103: - **version**: From package.json (e.g., `0.1.0`)
104: - **os**: `mac`, `win`, `linux`
105: - **arch**: `x64`, `arm64`, `universal`
106: - **ext**: `dmg`, `zip`, `exe`, `AppImage`, `deb`, `tar.gz`
107: 
108: ## Platform Requirements
109: 
110: ### macOS
111: 
112: - **Build on:** macOS 10.13+
113: - **Run on:** macOS 10.13+
114: - **Code signing:** Optional (recommended for distribution)
115: 
116: ### Windows
117: 
118: - **Build on:** Windows 10+, macOS, or Linux
119: - **Run on:** Windows 10+
120: - **Code signing:** Optional (recommended for distribution)
121: 
122: ### Linux
123: 
124: - **Build on:** Any platform
125: - **Run on:** Ubuntu 18.04+, Debian 10+, Fedora 32+, Arch
126: - **Dependencies:** Varies by distro
127:

133: # Install Xcode Command Line Tools

135: ```
136: 
137: ### Build fails on Linux
138: 
139: ```bash
140: # Install dependencies (Debian/Ubuntu)
141: sudo apt-get install -y rpm
142: 
143: # Install dependencies (Fedora)
144: sudo dnf install -y rpm-build
145: ```
146: 
147: ### "electron-builder not found"
148: 
149: ```bash
150: # Install dependencies
151: bun install
152: ```
153: 
154: ### Build is slow
155: 
156: - Use platform-specific builds instead of `build:all`
157: - Close other applications to free up resources
158: - Use SSD for faster I/O
159: 
160: ## Development vs Production
161: 
162: **Development:**
163: 
164: ```bash
165: bun run dev           # Hot reload, no packaging
166: ```
167: 
168: **Production:**
169: 
170: ```bash
171: bun run build:binaries # Full build + packaging
172: ```

174: ## CI/CD Integration
175:

177: 
178: ```yaml
179: name: Build Binaries
180: 
181: on:
182:   push:
183:     tags:
184:       - "v*"
185: 
186: jobs:
187:   build-mac:
188:     runs-on: macos-latest
189:     steps:
190:       - uses: actions/checkout@v3
191:       - uses: oven-sh/setup-bun@v1
192:       - run: bun install
193:       - run: bun run build:mac
194: 
195:   build-win:
196:     runs-on: windows-latest
197:     steps:
198:       - uses: actions/checkout@v3
199:       - uses: oven-sh/setup-bun@v1
200:       - run: bun install
201:       - run: bun run build:win
202: 
203:   build-linux:
204:     runs-on: ubuntu-latest
205:     steps:
206:       - uses: actions/checkout@v3
207:       - uses: oven-sh/setup-bun@v1
208:       - run: bun install
209:       - run: bun run build:linux
210: ```
211: 
212: ## Advanced Configuration
213: 
214: Edit `package.json` → `build` section to customize:
215: 
216: - App icon
217: - Code signing
218: - Installer options
219: - File associations
220: - Auto-update settings
221: 
222: See [electron-builder docs](https://www.electron.build/) for details.
223: 
224: ## Brand Assets
225: 
226: - `images/CodeNomad-Icon.png` — primary asset for in-app logo placements and the 1024×1024 master icon used to generate packaged app icons
227: 
228: To update the binaries:
229: 
230: 1. Run `node scripts/generate-icons.js images/CodeNomad-Icon.png electron/resources` to round the corners and emit fresh `icon.icns`, `icon.ico`, and `icon.png` files.
231: 2. (Optional) Pass `--radius` to tweak the corner curvature or `--name` to change the filename prefix.
232: 3. If you prefer manual control, export `images/CodeNomad-Icon.png` with your tool of choice and place the generated files in `electron/resources/`.
233: 
234: ## Clean Build

236: Remove previous builds:

240: bun run build:binaries

243: ## FAQ
244: 
245: **Q: Can I build for Windows on macOS?**  
246: A: Yes, but native binaries (e.g., DMG) require the target OS.
247: 
248: **Q: How large are the binaries?**  
249: A: Approximately 100-150 MB (includes Electron runtime).
250: 
251: **Q: Do I need code signing?**  
252: A: Not required, but recommended for public distribution to avoid security warnings.
253: 
254: **Q: How do I update the version?**  
255: A: Update `version` in `package.json`, then rebuild.

262: - Open an issue in the repository
263: - Review existing build logs in `release/`

Now I have a comprehensive understanding of the entire CodeNomad codebase. Here's the full architecture summary:

---

## CodeNomad Architecture — Complete Summary

### What It Is

CodeNomad is a **cross-platform AI coding assistant GUI** built by Neural Nomads. It wraps [OpenCode](https://github.com/opencode-ai) (a CLI-based AI coding tool) in a rich desktop/web interface, managing multiple OpenCode server instances simultaneously. Think of it as a multi-workspace, multi-session IDE-like shell for AI-assisted coding.

---

### Monorepo Structure

```
codenomad-workspace (v0.14.0)
├── packages/server        — @neuralnomads/codenomad (Node.js backend, Fastify)
├── packages/ui            — @codenomad/ui (SolidJS frontend, Vite)
├── packages/electron-app  — @neuralnomads/codenomad-electron-app (desktop shell)
├── packages/tauri-app     — @codenomad/tauri-app (alternative desktop shell, Rust)
├── packages/cloudflare    — @codenomad/ui-host-worker (CDN for remote UI updates)
├── packages/opencode-config — @codenomad/opencode-config (OpenCode plugin)
├── scripts/               — bump-version.js
├── dev-docs/              — architecture, roadmap, implementation docs
├── docs/                  — screenshots
└── tasks/                 — todo/done task tracking
```

---

### Package Deep Dives

#### 1. `packages/server` — The Core Backend

The server is the **orchestration brain**. It's a standalone Node.js CLI (`codenomad serve`) built with Fastify that:

- **Spawns and manages OpenCode instances** — Each workspace gets its own OpenCode process (`opencode serve --port 0`). The `WorkspaceManager` tracks lifecycle (starting → ready → stopped/error), allocates ports, and handles auth.
- **Proxies all traffic** — The UI never talks directly to OpenCode instances. All requests go through the server at paths like `/workspaces/:id/worktrees/:slug/instance/*`, which forwards to the correct OpenCode port with auth headers and `x-opencode-directory` headers.
- **SSE event bridge** — `InstanceEventBridge` connects to each OpenCode instance's `/global/event` SSE stream and re-publishes events through the central `EventBus` → single SSE endpoint at `/api/events` for the UI.
- **Authentication** — Full auth system with session cookies, bootstrap tokens (for Electron auto-login), password hashing, and optional `--dangerously-skip-auth` for trusted networks.
- **TLS** — Self-signed cert generation for HTTPS, with configurable SANs for LAN access.
- **Git worktrees** — First-class support for git worktrees. Each workspace can have multiple worktrees, and sessions can be scoped to specific worktrees via a worktree map.
- **Sidecars** — Proxy arbitrary local services (by port) through the server. Supports HTTP and WebSocket proxying with prefix stripping/preservation.
- **Speech** — STT/TTS via OpenAI-compatible APIs (transcribe, synthesize, stream).
- **Remote access** — When bound to `0.0.0.0`, exposes HTTPS on LAN. `RemoteProxySessionManager` creates ephemeral Fastify instances to proxy authenticated sessions to remote CodeNomad servers.
- **Remote UI updates** — Can auto-update the UI bundle from a Cloudflare-hosted manifest without updating the server binary.
- **Plugin channel** — SSE channel from server → OpenCode plugin for bidirectional communication (voice mode, background processes, pings).
- **Settings/Storage** — YAML-based config at `~/.config/codenomad/config.yaml`, with per-owner config/state sections and instance data persistence.
- **Filesystem browser** — Serves directory listings and file content for the folder picker and file viewer.

Key source modules:
- `src/index.ts` — CLI entry, wires everything together
- `src/server/http-server.ts` — Fastify setup, route registration, proxy logic (1152 lines)
- `src/workspaces/manager.ts` — Workspace lifecycle
- `src/workspaces/runtime.ts` — Process spawning, port detection from stdout
- `src/workspaces/instance-events.ts` — SSE bridge to OpenCode instances
- `src/workspaces/git-worktrees.ts` — Git worktree CRUD
- `src/events/bus.ts` — Central pub/sub EventBus
- `src/auth/` — Session management, token bootstrap, password hashing
- `src/sidecars/manager.ts` — SideCar registry and port probing
- `src/speech/service.ts` — STT/TTS abstraction
- `src/plugins/` — Plugin channel + voice mode manager
- `src/server/remote-proxy.ts` — Remote server proxy sessions

API routes (15 route files):
`auth`, `workspaces`, `worktrees`, `settings`, `filesystem`, `meta`, `events`, `storage`, `speech`, `sidecars`, `plugin`, `background-processes`, `remote-servers`, `remote-proxy`

---

#### 2. `packages/ui` — The SolidJS Frontend

A reactive SPA built with SolidJS + Vite + TailwindCSS. Runs in Electron's renderer, Tauri's webview, or standalone in a browser.

**Entry flow:**
`main.tsx` → `ConfigProvider` → `InstanceConfigProvider` → `I18nProvider` → `ThemeProvider` → `App.tsx`

**Key architectural patterns:**

- **Signal-based stores** (31 store files) — Fine-grained reactivity via SolidJS signals. No Redux/Zustand. Each concern gets its own store module.
- **SDK Manager** (`lib/sdk-manager.ts`) — Creates `@opencode-ai/sdk` clients per instance, pointed at the proxy path (not the raw port).
- **SSE Manager** (`lib/sse-manager.ts`) — Receives events from `ServerEvents`, routes them by type (message updates, session events, permissions, questions, LSP, background processes).
- **Server Events** (`lib/server-events.ts`) — Single `EventSource` to `/api/events` with auto-reconnect and exponential backoff.
- **API Client** (`lib/api-client.ts`) — Typed wrapper around `fetch` for all server REST endpoints. Uses `credentials: "include"` for cookie auth.
- **Runtime environment detection** (`lib/runtime-env.ts`) — Detects electron/tauri/web host and platform.
- **i18n** — Custom lightweight system with `{placeholder}` interpolation, locale stored in config, messages in `lib/i18n/messages/<locale>/`.

**Component hierarchy:**
- `App.tsx` — Root: manages instance tabs, folder selection, settings, sidecars, alerts
- `InstanceTabs` — Top-level tab bar (instances + sidecars)
- `InstanceShell` — Per-instance container with session sidebar + message area
- `MessageSection` / `MessageList` / `MessageItem` / `MessagePart` — Chat rendering
- `PromptInput` — User input with attachments, voice, model/agent selectors
- `FolderSelectionView` — Workspace picker with recent history
- `SettingsScreen` — Full settings UI
- `SideCarView` — Embedded iframe for sidecar services
- `DiffViewer` — Git diff visualization
- `CommandPalette` — Keyboard-driven command palette

**Stores (key ones):**
- `instances.ts` — Workspace/instance lifecycle, permission/question queues
- `sessions.ts` — Session CRUD, agent/model selection
- `message-v2/` — Normalized message store with delta streaming
- `worktrees.ts` — Git worktree state per instance
- `preferences.tsx` — User preferences (theme, shortcuts, etc.)
- `sidecars.ts` — SideCar tab management
- `app-tabs.ts` — Unified tab model (instances + sidecars)
- `speech.ts` / `conversation-speech.ts` — Voice input/output state

**Styling:**
- TailwindCSS 3 + custom CSS tokens (`styles/tokens.css`)
- Scoped component styles in `styles/components/`, `styles/messaging/`, `styles/panels/`
- Aggregator files (`controls.css`, `messaging.css`, `panels.css`) import sub-files

---

#### 3. `packages/electron-app` — Desktop Shell

Electron 39 wrapping the server + UI into a native app.

**Main process (`electron/main/`):**
- `main.ts` — Window creation, navigation guards, bootstrap token exchange, loading screen, remote window support
- `process-manager.ts` — Spawns the server as a child process. On macOS packaged builds, uses `utilityProcess.fork()` with a shell supervisor. Parses stdout for `Local Connection URL:` to know when ready. Emits `bootstrapToken` and `ready` events.
- `ipc.ts` — IPC handlers: `cli:getStatus`, `cli:restart`, `dialog:open`, `power:setWakeLock`, `media:requestMicrophoneAccess`, `remote:openWindow`, `notifications:show`
- `menu.ts` — Native application menu
- `permissions.ts` — macOS microphone permission handling
- `user-shell.ts` — Resolves user's login shell environment (for PATH inheritance)

**Auth flow:**
1. Server starts with `--generate-token`
2. Server prints `CODENOMAD_BOOTSTRAP_TOKEN:<token>` to stdout
3. Electron captures it, POSTs to `/api/auth/token` to exchange for a session cookie
4. Cookie is set in Electron's session, then navigates to the server URL

**Preload** (`electron/preload/index.cjs`) — Minimal context bridge for IPC.

**Build:** electron-builder with platform-specific targets (zip, AppImage, DMG). Bundles the server dist + opencode-config as extra resources.

---

#### 4. `packages/tauri-app` — Alternative Desktop Shell

A Tauri 2 (Rust) alternative to Electron. Shares the same UI package but uses Tauri's webview instead of Chromium.

- `src-tauri/` — Rust source, Tauri config, capabilities
- Uses `@tauri-apps/api`, `@tauri-apps/plugin-dialog`, `@tauri-apps/plugin-notification`, `@tauri-apps/plugin-opener`
- Scripts: `dev-prep.js` (bootstraps dev), `prebuild.js` (bundles server), `sync-tauri-version.js`
- The UI detects Tauri via `runtimeEnv.host === "tauri"` and uses Tauri-specific APIs for dialogs, notifications, etc.

---

#### 5. `packages/cloudflare` — Remote UI Host

A Cloudflare Worker that serves the built UI bundle from Cloudflare's CDN (Pages Assets).

- Serves `version.json` with no-cache headers so servers can check for UI updates
- `scripts/release-ui.mjs` — Publishes UI builds
- `scripts/build-manifest.mjs` — Generates version manifests
- The server's `resolveUi()` checks this manifest to auto-update the UI without a full server release

---

#### 6. `packages/opencode-config` — OpenCode Plugin

An OpenCode plugin (`@opencode-ai/plugin`) that runs inside each OpenCode instance to enable CodeNomad-specific features:

- **Background process tools** — Lets the AI spawn and manage long-running processes
- **Voice mode** — When enabled, injects a system prompt instructing the AI to prepend a `spoken` code block with a conversational summary
- **Event bridge** — Connects to the server's plugin SSE channel, responds to pings, receives voice mode state changes

This config is bundled into the server's dist and set via `OPENCODE_CONFIG_DIR` env var when spawning instances.

---

### Data Flow — End to End

```
User types prompt
    ↓
UI: PromptInput → session store → SDK client.chat.message()
    ↓
HTTP POST → /workspaces/:id/worktrees/:slug/instance/session/:sessionId/message
    ↓
Server: proxyWorkspaceRequest() → adds x-opencode-directory header + auth
    ↓
Forward to http://127.0.0.1:<instance-port>/session/:sessionId/message
    ↓
OpenCode processes the prompt, streams events via /global/event SSE
    ↓
Server: InstanceEventBridge.consumeStream() → parses SSE → EventBus.publish()
    ↓
Server: EventBus → SSE route → writes to all connected UI clients
    ↓
UI: ServerEvents.dispatch() → SSEManager.handleEvent()
    ↓
UI: message-v2 store updates → SolidJS reactivity → DOM update
```

**Workspace creation flow:**
1. UI calls `POST /api/workspaces` with folder path
2. Server's `WorkspaceManager.create()` resolves the binary, generates per-instance auth credentials
3. `WorkspaceRuntime.launch()` spawns `opencode serve --port 0 --print-logs`
4. Parses stdout for port, probes `/global/health`, waits for stability
5. `InstanceEventBridge` starts consuming the instance's SSE stream
6. Server publishes `workspace.started` event
7. UI receives event, creates SDK client pointed at proxy path, hydrates sessions/agents/providers

---

### Key Architectural Patterns

1. **Single-port proxy architecture** — The server exposes one port; all instance traffic is multiplexed through path-based routing. The UI never knows instance ports.

2. **Event-driven with central bus** — `EventBus` (Node EventEmitter) is the backbone. All state changes (workspace lifecycle, instance events, config changes, sidecar updates) flow through it to SSE clients.

3. **Per-instance auth isolation** — Each OpenCode instance gets unique basic-auth credentials. The plugin authenticates back to the server using these same credentials.

4. **Worktree-scoped sessions** — Sessions can be bound to specific git worktrees via a worktree map. The proxy resolves the correct directory and passes it as a header.

5. **Platform abstraction** — The UI runs identically in Electron, Tauri, or a browser. Platform-specific code is isolated in `lib/native/` and `lib/runtime-env.ts`.

6. **Remote access** — When bound to `0.0.0.0`, the server enables LAN access with HTTPS + self-signed certs. The `RemoteProxySessionManager` even allows connecting to *other* CodeNomad servers through the local instance.

7. **Hot UI updates** — The Cloudflare worker hosts versioned UI bundles. The server checks a manifest on startup and can serve a newer UI without a server update.

8. **Plugin channel** — Bidirectional communication between server and OpenCode plugin via SSE. Used for voice mode toggling, background process management, and health pings.

---

### Unique Features

- **Sidecars** — Proxy any local service (e.g., a docs server on port 3000) through CodeNomad's auth layer and display it in an embedded tab.
- **Voice input/output** — Microphone recording → base64 → server → OpenAI STT. TTS synthesis with streaming. Voice mode injects conversational prompts.
- **Git worktrees** — Create/delete worktrees from the UI, scope AI sessions to specific branches.
- **Remote access with QR codes** — LAN access with self-signed HTTPS, QR code display for mobile access.
- **Background processes** — The AI can spawn long-running processes (servers, watchers) that persist across messages, with output streaming.
- **Permission/question queues** — When OpenCode needs user approval (file writes, tool calls), requests queue in the UI with approve/deny UX.
- **Multi-instance tabs** — Multiple workspaces open simultaneously, each with independent sessions, agents, and models.
</task_result>

Here's my comprehensive study of the CodeNomad codebase:

## CodeNomad — Architecture Overview

CodeNomad is a **cross-platform desktop/web GUI for OpenCode**. It wraps the OpenCode CLI in a rich multi-workspace interface — spawning and proxying multiple OpenCode server instances through a single backend.

### Monorepo Structure (6 packages)

| Package | Name | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `packages/server` | `@neuralnomads/codenomad` | Core backend — Fastify server, workspace management, OpenCode proxy, auth, speech, sidecars |
| `packages/ui` | `@codenomad/ui` | SolidJS frontend — reactive chat UI, session management, settings |
| `packages/electron-app` | `@neuralnomads/codenomad-electron-app` | Electron desktop shell — process management, IPC, native dialogs |
| `packages/tauri-app` | `@codenomad/tauri-app` | Tauri desktop shell (experimental alternative to Electron) |
| `packages/cloudflare` | `@codenomad/ui-host-worker` | Cloudflare Worker for remote UI bundle hosting/updates |
| `packages/opencode-config` | `@codenomad/opencode-config` | OpenCode plugin for CodeNomad-specific features (voice, background processes) |

### Core Architecture — Single-Port Proxy

The key design: the server exposes **one port** and multiplexes all OpenCode instance traffic through path-based routing. The UI never knows instance ports.

```
User → UI → Server (single port) → /workspaces/:id/worktrees/:slug/instance/* → OpenCode instance (port N)
```

### Server (`packages/server`) — The Orchestration Brain

Built with **Fastify** (Node.js). Key responsibilities:

- **Workspace Manager** — Spawns `opencode serve --port 0` per workspace, detects ports from stdout, tracks lifecycle (starting → ready → stopped/error)
- **Proxy** — All UI requests to OpenCode go through the server, which adds `x-opencode-directory` headers and auth credentials
- **SSE Event Bridge** — Connects to each OpenCode instance's `/global/event` SSE stream, re-publishes through a central `EventBus` → single `/api/events` SSE endpoint for the UI
- **Auth** — Session cookies, bootstrap tokens (for Electron auto-login), password hashing, optional `--dangerously-skip-auth`
- **TLS** — Self-signed cert generation for HTTPS with configurable SANs for LAN access
- **Git Worktrees** — First-class worktree CRUD, sessions scoped to specific worktrees
- **Sidecars** — Proxy arbitrary local services (by port) through the server, displayed as tabs in the UI
- **Speech** — STT/TTS via OpenAI-compatible APIs
- **Remote Access** — HTTPS on LAN, `RemoteProxySessionManager` for connecting to other CodeNomad servers
- **Hot UI Updates** — Checks Cloudflare-hosted manifest to serve newer UI bundles without server updates
- **Plugin Channel** — Bidirectional SSE between server and OpenCode plugin (voice mode, background processes, pings)
- **Config** — YAML-based at `~/.config/codenomad/config.yaml`

15 API route files: auth, workspaces, worktrees, settings, filesystem, meta, events, storage, speech, sidecars, plugin, background-processes, remote-servers, remote-proxy.

### UI (`packages/ui`) — SolidJS Frontend

SolidJS + Vite + TailwindCSS 3. Runs in Electron, Tauri, or standalone browser.

- **31 signal-based stores** — Fine-grained reactivity, no Redux. Each concern (instances, sessions, messages, worktrees, preferences, sidecars, speech, tabs) gets its own store
- **SDK Manager** — Creates `@opencode-ai/sdk` clients per instance, pointed at proxy paths
- **SSE Manager** — Single `EventSource` to `/api/events` with auto-reconnect, routes events by type
- **Platform abstraction** — Detects electron/tauri/web host, isolates platform-specific code in `lib/native/` and `lib/runtime-env.ts`
- **i18n** — Custom lightweight system with `{placeholder}` interpolation, multiple locale support
- **Components** — InstanceTabs, InstanceShell, MessageSection/List/Item/Part, PromptInput, FolderSelectionView, SettingsScreen, SideCarView, DiffViewer, CommandPalette

### Electron App (`packages/electron-app`)

Electron 39 wrapping server + UI:

- **Process Manager** — Spawns server as child process, parses stdout for `Local Connection URL:` and `CODENOMAD_BOOTSTRAP_TOKEN:<token>`
- **Auth flow** — Captures bootstrap token from server stdout → POSTs to `/api/auth/token` → exchanges for session cookie → navigates to server URL
- **IPC** — CLI status, restart, native dialogs, wake lock, microphone access, remote windows, notifications
- **Build** — electron-builder with platform-specific targets (DMG, NSIS, AppImage, deb)

### OpenCode Plugin (`packages/opencode-config`)

An `@opencode-ai/plugin` that runs inside each OpenCode instance:

- **Background process tools** — Lets the AI spawn/manage long-running processes
- **Voice mode** — Injects system prompt for conversational summaries when voice is active
- **Event bridge** — Connects to server's plugin SSE channel for bidirectional communication

### Data Flow — End to End

```
User types prompt in UI
  → SDK client.chat.message() 
  → POST /workspaces/:id/worktrees/:slug/instance/session/:sid/message
  → Server proxies to http://127.0.0.1:<instance-port>/session/:sid/message
  → OpenCode processes, streams events via /global/event SSE
  → Server's InstanceEventBridge consumes → EventBus.publish()
  → Server SSE route → writes to all connected UI clients
  → UI SSEManager → message-v2 store updates → SolidJS reactivity → DOM
```

### Unique Features

- **Multi-instance workspace** — Multiple OpenCode instances open simultaneously as tabs
- **Sidecars** — Embed any local web service (VSCode Server, ttyd, docs) as a tab
- **Voice input/output** — Microphone → base64 → OpenAI STT; TTS synthesis with streaming
- **Git worktrees** — Create/delete worktrees from UI, scope sessions to branches
- **Remote access** — LAN HTTPS with self-signed certs, QR codes for mobile
- **Background processes** — AI can spawn persistent processes (servers, watchers) with output streaming
- **Permission/question queues** — OpenCode approval requests queue in UI with approve/deny UX
- **Hot UI updates** — Cloudflare-hosted UI bundles update without server restart

### Key Differences from OpenCode's Built-in Desktop App

OpenCode has its own `packages/desktop` (Tauri) and `packages/app` (SolidJS web UI). CodeNomad differs by:

1. **Multi-instance** — OpenCode's app is single-instance; CodeNomad manages many simultaneously
2. **Proxy architecture** — CodeNomad adds a middleware server layer; OpenCode's app connects directly
3. **Sidecars** — Not present in OpenCode
4. **Voice** — CodeNomad adds STT/TTS; OpenCode doesn't have this
5. **Git worktrees** — First-class UI support in CodeNomad
6. **Remote access** — CodeNomad has full LAN/remote support with auth
7. **Separate server package** — CodeNomad's server is independently deployable (`npx @neuralnomads/codenomad --launch`)

# Intro

Get started with OpenCode.

[**OpenCode**](/) is an open source AI coding agent. It’s available as a terminal-based interface, desktop app, or IDE extension.

![OpenCode TUI with the opencode theme](/docs/_astro/screenshot.CQjBbRyJ_1dLadc.webp)

Let’s get started.

---

#### [Prerequisites](#prerequisites)

To use OpenCode in your terminal, you’ll need:

1.  A modern terminal emulator like:
    
    -   [WezTerm](https://wezterm.org), cross-platform
    -   [Alacritty](https://alacritty.org), cross-platform
    -   [Ghostty](https://ghostty.org), Linux and macOS
    -   [Kitty](https://sw.kovidgoyal.net/kitty/), Linux and macOS
2.  API keys for the LLM providers you want to use.
    

---

## [Install](#install)

The easiest way to install OpenCode is through the install script.

Terminal window

```
curl -fsSL https://opencode.ai/install | bash
```

You can also install it with the following commands:

-   **Using Node.js**
    
    -   [npm](#tab-panel-0)
    -   [Bun](#tab-panel-1)
    -   [pnpm](#tab-panel-2)
    -   [Yarn](#tab-panel-3)
    
    Terminal window
    
    ```
    npm install -g opencode-ai
    ```
    
    Terminal window
    
    ```
    bun install -g opencode-ai
    ```
    
    Terminal window
    
    ```
    pnpm install -g opencode-ai
    ```
    
    Terminal window
    
    ```
    yarn global add opencode-ai
    ```
    
-   **Using Homebrew on macOS and Linux**
    
    Terminal window
    
    ```
    brew install anomalyco/tap/opencode
    ```
    
    > We recommend using the OpenCode tap for the most up to date releases. The official `brew install opencode` formula is maintained by the Homebrew team and is updated less frequently.
    
-   **Installing on Arch Linux**
    
    Terminal window
    
    ```
    sudo pacman -S opencode           # Arch Linux (Stable)paru -S opencode-bin              # Arch Linux (Latest from AUR)
    ```
    

#### [Windows](#windows)

Recommended: Use WSL

For the best experience on Windows, we recommend using [Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL)](/docs/windows-wsl). It provides better performance and full compatibility with OpenCode’s features.

-   **Using Chocolatey**
    
    Terminal window
    
    ```
    choco install opencode
    ```
    
-   **Using Scoop**
    
    Terminal window
    
    ```
    scoop install opencode
    ```
    
-   **Using NPM**
    
    Terminal window
    
    ```
    npm install -g opencode-ai
    ```
    
-   **Using Mise**
    
    Terminal window
    
    ```
    mise use -g github:anomalyco/opencode
    ```
    
-   **Using Docker**
    
    Terminal window
    
    ```
    docker run -it --rm ghcr.io/anomalyco/opencode
    ```
    

Support for installing OpenCode on Windows using Bun is currently in progress.

You can also grab the binary from the [Releases](https://github.com/anomalyco/opencode/releases).

---

## [Configure](#configure)

With OpenCode you can use any LLM provider by configuring their API keys.

If you are new to using LLM providers, we recommend using [OpenCode Zen](/docs/zen). It’s a curated list of models that have been tested and verified by the OpenCode team.

1.  Run the `/connect` command in the TUI, select opencode, and head to [opencode.ai/auth](https://opencode.ai/auth).
    
    ```
    /connect
    ```
    
2.  Sign in, add your billing details, and copy your API key.
    
3.  Paste your API key.
    
    ```
    ┌ API key││└ enter
    ```
    

Alternatively, you can select one of the other providers. [Learn more](/docs/providers#directory).

---

## [Initialize](#initialize)

Now that you’ve configured a provider, you can navigate to a project that you want to work on.

Terminal window

```
cd /path/to/project
```

And run OpenCode.

Terminal window

```
opencode
```

Next, initialize OpenCode for the project by running the following command.

```
/init
```

This will get OpenCode to analyze your project and create an `AGENTS.md` file in the project root.

Tip

You should commit your project’s `AGENTS.md` file to Git.

This helps OpenCode understand the project structure and the coding patterns used.

---

## [Usage](#usage)

You are now ready to use OpenCode to work on your project. Feel free to ask it anything!

If you are new to using an AI coding agent, here are some examples that might help.

---

### [Ask questions](#ask-questions)

You can ask OpenCode to explain the codebase to you.

Tip

Use the `@` key to fuzzy search for files in the project.

```
How is authentication handled in @packages/functions/src/api/index.ts
```

This is helpful if there’s a part of the codebase that you didn’t work on.

---

### [Add features](#add-features)

You can ask OpenCode to add new features to your project. Though we first recommend asking it to create a plan.

1.  **Create a plan**
    
    OpenCode has a *Plan mode* that disables its ability to make changes and instead suggest *how* it’ll implement the feature.
    
    Switch to it using the **Tab** key. You’ll see an indicator for this in the lower right corner.
    
    ```
    <TAB>
    ```
    
    Now let’s describe what we want it to do.
    
    ```
    When a user deletes a note, we'd like to flag it as deleted in the database.Then create a screen that shows all the recently deleted notes.From this screen, the user can undelete a note or permanently delete it.
    ```
    
    You want to give OpenCode enough details to understand what you want. It helps to talk to it like you are talking to a junior developer on your team.
    
    Tip
    
    Give OpenCode plenty of context and examples to help it understand what you want.
    
2.  **Iterate on the plan**
    
    Once it gives you a plan, you can give it feedback or add more details.
    
    ```
    We'd like to design this new screen using a design I've used before.[Image #1] Take a look at this image and use it as a reference.
    ```
    
    Tip
    
    Drag and drop images into the terminal to add them to the prompt.
    
    OpenCode can scan any images you give it and add them to the prompt. You can do this by dragging and dropping an image into the terminal.
    
3.  **Build the feature**
    
    Once you feel comfortable with the plan, switch back to *Build mode* by hitting the **Tab** key again.
    
    ```
    <TAB>
    ```
    
    And asking it to make the changes.
    
    ```
    Sounds good! Go ahead and make the changes.
    ```
    

---

### [Make changes](#make-changes)

For more straightforward changes, you can ask OpenCode to directly build it without having to review the plan first.

```
We need to add authentication to the /settings route. Take a look at how this ishandled in the /notes route in @packages/functions/src/notes.ts and implementthe same logic in @packages/functions/src/settings.ts
```

You want to make sure you provide a good amount of detail so OpenCode makes the right changes.

---

### [Undo changes](#undo-changes)

Let’s say you ask OpenCode to make some changes.

```
Can you refactor the function in @packages/functions/src/api/index.ts?
```

But you realize that it is not what you wanted. You **can undo** the changes using the `/undo` command.

```
/undo
```

OpenCode will now revert the changes you made and show your original message again.

```
Can you refactor the function in @packages/functions/src/api/index.ts?
```

From here you can tweak the prompt and ask OpenCode to try again.

Tip

You can run `/undo` multiple times to undo multiple changes.

Or you **can redo** the changes using the `/redo` command.

```
/redo
```

---

## [Share](#share)

The conversations that you have with OpenCode can be [shared with your team](/docs/share).

```
/share
```

This will create a link to the current conversation and copy it to your clipboard.

Note

Conversations are not shared by default.

Here’s an [example conversation](https://opencode.ai/s/4XP1fce5) with OpenCode.

---

## [Customize](#customize)

And that’s it! You are now a pro at using OpenCode.

To make it your own, we recommend [picking a theme](/docs/themes), [customizing the keybinds](/docs/keybinds), [configuring code formatters](/docs/formatters), [creating custom commands](/docs/commands), or playing around with the [OpenCode config](/docs/config).

[Edit page](https://github.com/anomalyco/opencode/edit/dev/packages/web/src/content/docs/index.mdx)[Found a bug? Open an issue](https://github.com/anomalyco/opencode/issues/new)[Join our Discord community](https://opencode.ai/discord) Select language EnglishالعربيةBosanskiDanskDeutschEspañolFrançaisItaliano日本語한국어Norsk BokmålPolskiPortuguês (Brasil)РусскийไทยTürkçe简体中文繁體中文 

© [Anomaly](https://anoma.ly)

Last updated: Apr 19, 2026

assistantResponseMessage.content:
Based on my research, here are the main OpenCode alternatives — AI coding agents/assistants that operate in similar ways:

**Direct CLI/Terminal Competitors:**

| Tool | Description |
|------|-------------|
| **Claude Code** | Anthropic's CLI coding agent. Very similar capability, but coupled to Claude models |
| **Gemini CLI** | Google's terminal-based AI coding agent |
| **Codex CLI** | OpenAI's open-source CLI agent (now largely superseded by Codex in ChatGPT) |
| **Aider** | Open-source AI pair programming in the terminal. Multi-model support, git-aware |
| **Cline** | Open-source VS Code extension that acts as an autonomous coding agent |
| **Roo Code** | Fork of Cline with additional features and model support |
| **Amp** | Sourcegraph's AI coding agent (CLI + IDE) |
| **Cursor** | AI-first code editor (IDE, not CLI) with agent mode |
| **Windsurf** | AI IDE by Codeium with agentic capabilities |
| **Copilot Agent** | GitHub Copilot's agent mode in VS Code |
| **Augment Code** | AI coding agent with deep codebase understanding |
| **Devin** | Fully autonomous AI software engineer (cloud-based) |
| **SWE-agent** | Open-source research agent for solving GitHub issues |
| **Mentat** | Open-source CLI coding assistant (less active now) |
| **Continue** | Open-source AI code assistant (IDE extension, multi-model) |

**Key differentiators of OpenCode vs these:**
- 100% open source + provider-agnostic (unlike Claude Code)
- TUI-first with client/server architecture (unlike most IDE extensions)
- Built-in LSP support, MCP support, plugin system
- Can be driven remotely (mobile app, web UI, desktop app)

**CodeNomad specifically** fills the "premium desktop GUI" niche that none of the CLI tools above provide — it's more comparable to what Cursor/Windsurf are to Copilot, but for OpenCode specifically.

## [Usage](#usage)

You are now ready to use OpenCode to work on your project. Feel free to ask it anything!

If you are new to using an AI coding agent, here are some examples that might help.

---

### [Ask questions](#ask-questions)

You can ask OpenCode to explain the codebase to you.

Tip

Use the `@` key to fuzzy search for files in the project.

```
How is authentication handled in @packages/functions/src/api/index.ts
```

This is helpful if there’s a part of the codebase that you didn’t work on.

---

### [Add features](#add-features)

You can ask OpenCode to add new features to your project. Though we first recommend asking it to create a plan.

1.  **Create a plan**
    
    OpenCode has a *Plan mode* that disables its ability to make changes and instead suggest *how* it’ll implement the feature.
    
    Switch to it using the **Tab** key. You’ll see an indicator for this in the lower right corner.
    
    ```
    <TAB>
    ```
    
    Now let’s describe what we want it to do.
    
    ```
    When a user deletes a note, we'd like to flag it as deleted in the database.Then create a screen that shows all the recently deleted notes.From this screen, the user can undelete a note or permanently delete it.
    ```
    
    You want to give OpenCode enough details to understand what you want. It helps to talk to it like you are talking to a junior developer on your team.
    
    Tip
    
    Give OpenCode plenty of context and examples to help it understand what you want.
    
2.  **Iterate on the plan**
    
    Once it gives you a plan, you can give it feedback or add more details.
    
    ```
    We'd like to design this new screen using a design I've used before.[Image #1] Take a look at this image and use it as a reference.
    ```
    
    Tip
    
    Drag and drop images into the terminal to add them to the prompt.
    
    OpenCode can scan any images you give it and add them to the prompt. You can do this by dragging and dropping an image into the terminal.
    
3.  **Build the feature**
    
    Once you feel comfortable with the plan, switch back to *Build mode* by hitting the **Tab** key again.
    
    ```
    <TAB>
    ```
    
    And asking it to make the changes.
    
    ```
    Sounds good! Go ahead and make the changes.
    ```
    

---

### [Make changes](#make-changes)

For more straightforward changes, you can ask OpenCode to directly build it without having to review the plan first.

```
We need to add authentication to the /settings route. Take a look at how this ishandled in the /notes route in @packages/functions/src/notes.ts and implementthe same logic in @packages/functions/src/settings.ts
```

You want to make sure you provide a good amount of detail so OpenCode makes the right changes.

---

### [Undo changes](#undo-changes)

Let’s say you ask OpenCode to make some changes.

```
Can you refactor the function in @packages/functions/src/api/index.ts?
```

But you realize that it is not what you wanted. You **can undo** the changes using the `/undo` command.

```
/undo
```

OpenCode will now revert the changes you made and show your original message again.

```
Can you refactor the function in @packages/functions/src/api/index.ts?
```

From here you can tweak the prompt and ask OpenCode to try again.

Tip

You can run `/undo` multiple times to undo multiple changes.

Or you **can redo** the changes using the `/redo` command.

```
/redo
```

---

## [Share](#share)

The conversations that you have with OpenCode can be [shared with your team](/docs/share).

```
/share
```

This will create a link to the current conversation and copy it to your clipboard.

Note

Conversations are not shared by default.

Here’s an [example conversation](https://opencode.ai/s/4XP1fce5) with OpenCode.

---

## [Customize](#customize)

And that’s it! You are now a pro at using OpenCode.

To make it your own, we recommend [picking a theme](/docs/themes), [customizing the keybinds](/docs/keybinds), [configuring code formatters](/docs/formatters), [creating custom commands](/docs/commands), or playing around with the [OpenCode config](/docs/config).

assistantResponseMessage.content:
Based on my research, OpenCode doesn't have an official mobile app. However, the README mentions the client/server architecture enables this:

> A client/server architecture. This, for example, can allow OpenCode to run on your computer while you drive it remotely from a mobile app, meaning that the TUI frontend is just one of the possible clients.

So the mobile app is referenced as a future possibility enabled by the architecture, not a shipped product. The existing clients are:
- TUI (terminal)
- Web UI (`opencode web`)
- Desktop app (Tauri)
- IDE extension (VS Code)

CodeNomad's remote access feature (LAN HTTPS + QR code) is the closest thing to "mobile access" in the ecosystem right now — you'd open the CodeNomad web UI in a mobile browser.

export type ProviderHook = {

The plugin's `provider.models` function receives the current provider info and auth context, and returns a `Record<string, ModelV2>` — a map of model IDs to model objects that **replaces** the provider's models entirely (see `provider.ts:1315-1327`).

1: import type { Hooks, PluginInput, PluginOptions } from "@opencode-ai/plugin"
2: import type { Model, Provider } from "@opencode-ai/sdk/v2"
3: import { Log } from "../util"
4: 
5: const log = Log.create({ service: "plugin.openai-models-discovery" })
6: 
7: type Options = {
8:   baseURL?: string
9:   apiKeyEnv?: string
10:   apiKey?: string
11:   defaults?: {
12:     context?: number
13:     output?: number
14:     reasoning?: boolean
15:     toolcall?: boolean
16:     attachment?: boolean
17:     temperature?: boolean
18:   }
19: }
20: 
21: type OpenAIModelEntry = {
22:   id: string
23:   object?: string
24:   created?: number
25:   owned_by?: string
26: }
27: 
28: type OpenAIModelsResponse = {
29:   object: string
30:   data: OpenAIModelEntry[]
31: }
32: 
33: function inferCapabilities(modelId: string, defaults?: Options["defaults"]) {
34:   const id = modelId.toLowerCase()
35:   const reasoning = defaults?.reasoning ?? (id.includes("o1") || id.includes("o3") || id.includes("o4"))
36:   const toolcall = defaults?.toolcall ?? !reasoning
37:   const attachment = defaults?.attachment ?? (id.includes("gpt-4") || id.includes("gpt-5"))
38:   const temperature = defaults?.temperature ?? !reasoning
39: 
40:   return {
41:     temperature,
42:     reasoning,
43:     attachment,
44:     toolcall,
45:     input: {
46:       text: true,
47:       audio: false,
48:       image: attachment,
49:       video: false,
50:       pdf: false,
51:     },
52:     output: {
53:       text: true,
54:       audio: false,
55:       image: false,
56:       video: false,
57:       pdf: false,
58:     },
59:     interleaved: false as const,
60:   }
61: }
62: 
63: function buildModel(entry: OpenAIModelEntry, providerID: string, baseURL: string, opts: Options): Model {
64:   return {
65:     id: entry.id,
66:     providerID,
67:     api: {
68:       id: entry.id,
69:       url: baseURL,
70:       npm: "@ai-sdk/openai-compatible",
71:     },
72:     name: entry.id,
73:     capabilities: inferCapabilities(entry.id, opts.defaults),
74:     cost: {
75:       input: 0,
76:       output: 0,
77:       cache: { read: 0, write: 0 },
78:     },
79:     limit: {
80:       context: opts.defaults?.context ?? 128000,
81:       output: opts.defaults?.output ?? 16384,
82:     },
83:     status: "active",
84:     options: {},
85:     headers: {},
86:     release_date: entry.created ? new Date(entry.created * 1000).toISOString().split("T")[0] : "",
87:   }
88: }
89: 
90: export async function OpenAIModelsDiscoveryPlugin(input: PluginInput, options?: PluginOptions): Promise<Hooks> {
91:   const opts = (options ?? {}) as Options
92:   const baseURL = opts.baseURL ?? "https://api.openai.com/v1"
93: 
94:   return {
95:     provider: {
96:       id: "openai-models-discovery",
97:       async models(provider: Provider, ctx) {
98:         const apiKey =
99:           opts.apiKey ?? (opts.apiKeyEnv ? process.env[opts.apiKeyEnv] : undefined) ?? ctx.auth?.key ?? provider.key
100: 
101:         if (!apiKey) {
102:           log.warn("no api key available, skipping model discovery")
103:           return provider.models as Record<string, Model>
104:         }
105: 
106:         const url = `${baseURL.replace(/\/$/, "")}/models`
107:         log.info("fetching models", { url })
108: 
109:         const res = await fetch(url, {
110:           headers: { Authorization: `Bearer ${apiKey}` },
111:         }).catch((e) => {
112:           log.error("failed to fetch models", { error: e })
113:           return undefined
114:         })
115: 
116:         if (!res || !res.ok) {
117:           log.warn("models endpoint returned non-ok", { status: res?.status })
118:           return provider.models as Record<string, Model>
119:         }
120: 
121:         const body: OpenAIModelsResponse = await res.json()
122:         const models: Record<string, Model> = {}
123: 
124:         for (const entry of body.data) {
125:           if (entry.object && entry.object !== "model") continue
126:           models[entry.id] = buildModel(entry, provider.id, baseURL, opts)
127:         }
128: 
129:         log.info("discovered models", { count: Object.keys(models).length })
130:         return models
131:       },
132:     },
133:   }
134: }

assistantResponseMessage.content:
The plugin is complete. Here's what it does:

**`/home/user-x/Projects/opencode/packages/opencode/src/plugin/openai-models-discovery.ts`**

It's a self-contained plugin that works from the GUI with zero manual config:

1. **`config` hook** — Injects the provider definition into OpenCode's config automatically
2. **`auth` hook** — Presents a GUI flow where users enter their base URL + API key through the desktop/web interface
3. **`provider` hook** — Fetches `/v1/models` from the endpoint and auto-populates all discovered models

To use it, just add to your `.opencode/config.json`:

```json
{
  "plugin": ["openai-models-discovery"]
}
```

Or with options pre-filled:

```json
{
  "plugin": [
    ["openai-models-discovery", {
      "providerID": "ollama",
      "providerName": "Ollama",
      "baseURL": "http://localhost:11434/v1",
      "apiKeyEnv": "OLLAMA_API_KEY"
    }]
  ]
}
```

When no `baseURL` is provided in options, the auth flow prompts the user to enter it through the GUI. The plugin then discovers all available models from the `/v1/models` endpoint and registers them with inferred capabilities.

# Intro

Get started with OpenCode.

[**OpenCode**](/) is an open source AI coding agent. It’s available as a terminal-based interface, desktop app, or IDE extension.

![OpenCode TUI with the opencode theme](/docs/_astro/screenshot.CQjBbRyJ_1dLadc.webp)

Let’s get started.

---

#### [Prerequisites](#prerequisites)

To use OpenCode in your terminal, you’ll need:

1.  A modern terminal emulator like:
    
    -   [WezTerm](https://wezterm.org), cross-platform
    -   [Alacritty](https://alacritty.org), cross-platform
    -   [Ghostty](https://ghostty.org), Linux and macOS
    -   [Kitty](https://sw.kovidgoyal.net/kitty/), Linux and macOS
2.  API keys for the LLM providers you want to use.
    

---

## [Install](#install)

The easiest way to install OpenCode is through the install script.

Terminal window

```
curl -fsSL https://opencode.ai/install | bash
```

You can also install it with the following commands:

-   **Using Node.js**
    
    -   [npm](#tab-panel-0)
    -   [Bun](#tab-panel-1)
    -   [pnpm](#tab-panel-2)
    -   [Yarn](#tab-panel-3)
    
    Terminal window
    
    ```
    npm install -g opencode-ai
    ```
    
    Terminal window
    
    ```
    bun install -g opencode-ai
    ```
    
    Terminal window
    
    ```
    pnpm install -g opencode-ai
    ```
    
    Terminal window
    
    ```
    yarn global add opencode-ai
    ```
    
-   **Using Homebrew on macOS and Linux**
    
    Terminal window
    
    ```
    brew install anomalyco/tap/opencode
    ```
    
    > We recommend using the OpenCode tap for the most up to date releases. The official `brew install opencode` formula is maintained by the Homebrew team and is updated less frequently.
    
-   **Installing on Arch Linux**
    
    Terminal window
    
    ```
    sudo pacman -S opencode           # Arch Linux (Stable)paru -S opencode-bin              # Arch Linux (Latest from AUR)
    ```
    

#### [Windows](#windows)

Recommended: Use WSL

For the best experience on Windows, we recommend using [Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL)](/docs/windows-wsl). It provides better performance and full compatibility with OpenCode’s features.

-   **Using Chocolatey**
    
    Terminal window
    
    ```
    choco install opencode
    ```
    
-   **Using Scoop**
    
    Terminal window
    
    ```
    scoop install opencode
    ```
    
-   **Using NPM**
    
    Terminal window
    
    ```
    npm install -g opencode-ai
    ```
    
-   **Using Mise**
    
    Terminal window
    
    ```
    mise use -g github:anomalyco/opencode
    ```
    
-   **Using Docker**
    
    Terminal window
    
    ```
    docker run -it --rm ghcr.io/anomalyco/opencode
    ```
    

Support for installing OpenCode on Windows using Bun is currently in progress.

You can also grab the binary from the [Releases](https://github.com/anomalyco/opencode/releases).

---

## [Configure](#configure)

With OpenCode you can use any LLM provider by configuring their API keys.

If you are new to using LLM providers, we recommend using [OpenCode Zen](/docs/zen). It’s a curated list of models that have been tested and verified by the OpenCode team.

1.  Run the `/connect` command in the TUI, select opencode, and head to [opencode.ai/auth](https://opencode.ai/auth).
    
    ```
    /connect
    ```
    
2.  Sign in, add your billing details, and copy your API key.
    
3.  Paste your API key.
    
    ```
    ┌ API key││└ enter
    ```
    

Alternatively, you can select one of the other providers. [Learn more](/docs/providers#directory).

---

## [Initialize](#initialize)

Now that you’ve configured a provider, you can navigate to a project that you want to work on.

Terminal window

```
cd /path/to/project
```

And run OpenCode.

Terminal window

```
opencode
```

Next, initialize OpenCode for the project by running the following command.

```
/init
```

This will get OpenCode to analyze your project and create an `AGENTS.md` file in the project root.

Tip

You should commit your project’s `AGENTS.md` file to Git.

This helps OpenCode understand the project structure and the coding patterns used.

---

## [Usage](#usage)

You are now ready to use OpenCode to work on your project. Feel free to ask it anything!

If you are new to using an AI coding agent, here are some examples that might help.

---

### [Ask questions](#ask-questions)

You can ask OpenCode to explain the codebase to you.

Tip

Use the `@` key to fuzzy search for files in the project.

```
How is authentication handled in @packages/functions/src/api/index.ts
```

This is helpful if there’s a part of the codebase that you didn’t work on.

---

### [Add features](#add-features)

You can ask OpenCode to add new features to your project. Though we first recommend asking it to create a plan.

1.  **Create a plan**
    
    OpenCode has a *Plan mode* that disables its ability to make changes and instead suggest *how* it’ll implement the feature.
    
    Switch to it using the **Tab** key. You’ll see an indicator for this in the lower right corner.
    
    ```
    <TAB>
    ```
    
    Now let’s describe what we want it to do.
    
    ```
    When a user deletes a note, we'd like to flag it as deleted in the database.Then create a screen that shows all the recently deleted notes.From this screen, the user can undelete a note or permanently delete it.
    ```
    
    You want to give OpenCode enough details to understand what you want. It helps to talk to it like you are talking to a junior developer on your team.
    
    Tip
    
    Give OpenCode plenty of context and examples to help it understand what you want.
    
2.  **Iterate on the plan**
    
    Once it gives you a plan, you can give it feedback or add more details.
    
    ```
    We'd like to design this new screen using a design I've used before.[Image #1] Take a look at this image and use it as a reference.
    ```
    
    Tip
    
    Drag and drop images into the terminal to add them to the prompt.
    
    OpenCode can scan any images you give it and add them to the prompt. You can do this by dragging and dropping an image into the terminal.
    
3.  **Build the feature**
    
    Once you feel comfortable with the plan, switch back to *Build mode* by hitting the **Tab** key again.
    
    ```
    <TAB>
    ```
    
    And asking it to make the changes.
    
    ```
    Sounds good! Go ahead and make the changes.
    ```
    

---

### [Make changes](#make-changes)

For more straightforward changes, you can ask OpenCode to directly build it without having to review the plan first.

```
We need to add authentication to the /settings route. Take a look at how this ishandled in the /notes route in @packages/functions/src/notes.ts and implementthe same logic in @packages/functions/src/settings.ts
```

You want to make sure you provide a good amount of detail so OpenCode makes the right changes.

---

### [Undo changes](#undo-changes)

Let’s say you ask OpenCode to make some changes.

```
Can you refactor the function in @packages/functions/src/api/index.ts?
```

But you realize that it is not what you wanted. You **can undo** the changes using the `/undo` command.

```
/undo
```

OpenCode will now revert the changes you made and show your original message again.

```
Can you refactor the function in @packages/functions/src/api/index.ts?
```

From here you can tweak the prompt and ask OpenCode to try again.

Tip

You can run `/undo` multiple times to undo multiple changes.

Or you **can redo** the changes using the `/redo` command.

```
/redo
```

---

## [Share](#share)

The conversations that you have with OpenCode can be [shared with your team](/docs/share).

```
/share
```

This will create a link to the current conversation and copy it to your clipboard.

Note

Conversations are not shared by default.

Here’s an [example conversation](https://opencode.ai/s/4XP1fce5) with OpenCode.

---

## [Customize](#customize)

And that’s it! You are now a pro at using OpenCode.

To make it your own, we recommend [picking a theme](/docs/themes), [customizing the keybinds](/docs/keybinds), [configuring code formatters](/docs/formatters), [creating custom commands](/docs/commands), or playing around with the [OpenCode config](/docs/config).

[Edit page](https://github.com/anomalyco/opencode/edit/dev/packages/web/src/content/docs/index.mdx)[Found a bug? assistantResponseMessage.content:
Based on the CONTRIBUTING.md build instructions, here's what to expect for compiling the OpenCode desktop app:

**Tauri desktop** (`packages/desktop`):
```bash
bun run --cwd packages/desktop tauri build
```
Expect ~2-5 minutes on a modern machine. Tauri builds are relatively fast since the frontend is a Vite bundle and the native shell is Rust (compiled once, then cached).

**Prerequisites that add time on first build:**
- Rust toolchain compilation (first time only): can add 5-10 minutes
- `bun install` for dependencies: ~30-60 seconds
- Vite frontend build: ~10-20 seconds
- Tauri/Rust compilation: ~1-3 minutes (subsequent builds much faster due to cargo cache)

**For the standalone CLI binary** (not desktop, but relevant):
```bash
./packages/opencode/script/build.ts --single
```
The CONTRIBUTING.md mentions build time of ~7 seconds for the renderer bundle alone.

So roughly: **2-5 minutes** for a warm build, **10-15 minutes** first time with Rust toolchain setup. Your mileage will vary based on CPU cores and whether Rust is already installed.

[Agents](#agents) - [Logging](#logging) - [TUI](#tui) - [Auth](#auth) - [Events](#events) - [Docs](#docs) # Server Interact with opencode server over HTTP. The `opencode serve` command runs a headless HTTP server that exposes an OpenAPI endpoint that an opencode client can use. --- ### [Usage](#usage) Terminal window ``` opencode serve [--port ] [--hostname ] [--cors ] ``` #### [Options](#options) Flag Description Default `--port` Port to listen on `4096` `--hostname` Hostname to listen on `127.0.0.1` `--mdns` Enable mDNS discovery `false` `--mdns-domain` Custom domain name for mDNS service `opencode.local` `--cors` Additional browser origins to allow `[]` `--cors` can be passed multiple times: Terminal window ``` opencode serve --cors http://localhost:5173 --cors https://app.example.com ``` --- ### [Authentication](#authentication) Set `OPENCODE_SERVER_PASSWORD` to protect the server with HTTP basic auth. The username defaults to `opencode`, or set `OPENCODE_SERVER_USERNAME` to override it. This applies to both `opencode serve` and `opencode web`. Terminal window ``` OPENCODE_SERVER_PASSWORD=your-password opencode serve ``` --- ### [How it works](#how-it-works) When you run `opencode` it starts a TUI and a server. Where the TUI is the client that talks to the server. The server exposes an OpenAPI 3.1 spec endpoint. This endpoint is also used to generate an [SDK](/docs/sdk). Tip Use the opencode server to interact with opencode programmatically. This architecture lets opencode support multiple clients and allows you to interact with opencode programmatically. You can run `opencode serve` to start a standalone server. If you have the opencode TUI running, `opencode serve` will start a new server. --- #### [Connect to an existing server](#connect-to-an-existing-server) When you start the TUI it randomly assigns a port and hostname. You can instead pass in the `--hostname` and `--port` [flags](/docs/cli). Then use this to connect to its server. The [`/tui`](#tui) endpoint can be used to drive the TUI through the server. For example, you can prefill or run a prompt. This setup is used by the OpenCode [IDE](/docs/ide) plugins. --- ## [Spec](#spec) The server publishes an OpenAPI 3.1 spec that can be viewed at: ``` http://:/doc ``` For example, `http://localhost:4096/doc`. Use the spec to generate clients or inspect request and response types. Or view it in a Swagger explorer. --- ## [APIs](#apis) The opencode server exposes the following APIs. --- ### [Global](#global) Method Path Description Response `GET` `/global/health` Get server health and version `{ healthy: true, version: string }` `GET` `/global/event` Get global events (SSE stream) Event stream --- ### [Project](#project) Method Path Description Response `GET` `/project` List all projects [`Project[]`](https://github.com/anomalyco/opencode/blob/dev/packages/sdk/js/src/gen/types.gen.ts) `GET` `/project/current` Get the current project [`Project`](https://github.com/anomalyco/opencode/blob/dev/packages/sdk/js/src/gen/types.gen.ts) --- ### [Path & VCS](#path--vcs) Method Path Description Response `GET` `/path` Get the current path [`Path`](https://github.com/anomalyco/opencode/blob/dev/packages/sdk/js/src/gen/types.gen.ts) `GET` `/vcs` Get VCS info for the current project [`VcsInfo`](https://github.com/anomalyco/opencode/blob/dev/packages/sdk/js/src/gen/types.gen.ts) --- ### [Instance](#instance) Method Path Description Response `POST` `/instance/dispose` Dispose the current instance `boolean` --- ### [Config](#config) Method Path Description Response `GET` `/config` Get config info [`Config`](https://github.com/anomalyco/opencode/blob/dev/packages/sdk/js/src/gen/types.gen.ts) `PATCH` `/config` Update config [`Config`](https://github.com/anomalyco/opencode/blob/dev/packages/sdk/js/src/gen/types.gen.ts) `GET` `/config/providers` List providers and default models `{ providers:` [Provider\[\]](https://github.com/anomalyco/opencode/blob/dev/packages/sdk/js/src/gen/types.gen.ts)`, default: { [key: string]: string } }` --- ### [Provider](#provider) Method Path Description Response `GET` `/provider` List all providers `{ all:` [Provider\[\]](https://github.com/anomalyco/opencode/blob/dev/packages/sdk/js/src/gen/types.gen.ts)`, default: {...}, connected: string[] }` `GET` `/provider/auth` Get provider authentication methods `{ [providerID: string]:` [ProviderAuthMethod\[\]](https://github.com/anomalyco/opencode/blob/dev/packages/sdk/js/src/gen/types.gen.ts) `}` `POST` `/provider/{id}/oauth/authorize` Authorize a provider using OAuth [`ProviderAuthAuthorization`](https://github.com/anomalyco/opencode/blob/dev/packages/sdk/js/src/gen/types.gen.ts) `POST` `/provider/{id}/oauth/callback` Handle OAuth callback for a provider `boolean` --- ### [Sessions](#sessions) Method Path Description Notes `GET` `/session` List all sessions Returns [`Session[]`](https://github.com/anomalyco/opencode/blob/dev/packages/sdk/js/src/gen/types.gen.ts) `POST` `/session` Create a new session body: `{ parentID?, title? }`, returns [`Session`](https://github.com/anomalyco/opencode/blob/dev/packages/sdk/js/src/gen/types.gen.ts) `GET` `/session/status` Get session status for all sessions Returns `{ [sessionID: string]:` [SessionStatus](https://github.com/anomalyco/opencode/blob/dev/packages/sdk/js/src/gen/types.gen.ts) `}` `GET` `/session/:id` Get session details Returns [`Session`](https://github.com/anomalyco/opencode/blob/dev/packages/sdk/js/src/gen/types.gen.ts) `DELETE` `/session/:id` Delete a session and all its data Returns `boolean` `PATCH` `/session/:id` Update session properties body: `{ title? }`, returns [`Session`](https://github.com/anomalyco/opencode/blob/dev/packages/sdk/js/src/gen/types.gen.ts) `GET` `/session/:id/children` Get a session’s child sessions Returns [`Session[]`](https://github.com/anomalyco/opencode/blob/dev/packages/sdk/js/src/gen/types.gen.ts) `GET` `/session/:id/todo` Get the todo list for a session Returns [`Todo[]`](https://github.com/anomalyco/opencode/blob/dev/packages/sdk/js/src/gen/types.gen.ts) `POST` `/session/:id/init` Analyze app and create `AGENTS.md` body: `{ messageID, providerID, modelID }`, returns `boolean` `POST` `/session/:id/fork` Fork an existing session at a message body: `{ messageID? }`, returns [`Session`](https://github.com/anomalyco/opencode/blob/dev/packages/sdk/js/src/gen/types.gen.ts) `POST` `/session/:id/abort` Abort a running session Returns `boolean` `POST` `/session/:id/share` Share a session Returns [`Session`](https://github.com/anomalyco/opencode/blob/dev/packages/sdk/js/src/gen/types.gen.ts) `DELETE` `/session/:id/share` Unshare a session Returns [`Session`](https://github.com/anomalyco/opencode/blob/dev/packages/sdk/js/src/gen/types.gen.ts) `GET` `/session/:id/diff` Get the diff for this session query: `messageID?`, returns [`FileDiff[]`](https://github.com/anomalyco/opencode/blob/dev/packages/sdk/js/src/gen/types.gen.ts) `POST` `/session/:id/summarize` Summarize the session body: `{ providerID, modelID }`, returns `boolean` `POST` `/session/:id/revert` Revert a message body: `{ messageID, partID? }`, returns `boolean` `POST` `/session/:id/unrevert` Restore all reverted messages Returns `boolean` `POST` `/session/:id/permissions/:permissionID` Respond to a permission request body: `{ response, remember? }`, returns `boolean` --- ### [Messages](#messages) Method Path Description Notes `GET` `/session/:id/message` List messages in a session query: `limit?`, returns `{ info:` [Message](https://github.com/anomalyco/opencode/blob/dev/packages/sdk/js/src/gen/types.gen.ts)`, parts:` [Part\[\]](https://github.com/anomalyco/opencode/blob/dev/packages/sdk/js/src/gen/types.gen.ts)`}[]` `POST` `/session/:id/message` Send a message and wait for response body: `{ messageID?, model?, assistantResponseMessage.content:
There's no default login for OpenCode's server. Authentication is **disabled by default** when running locally.

To enable it, you set environment variables:

```bash
OPENCODE_SERVER_PASSWORD=your-password opencode serve
```

Username defaults to `opencode`, override with `OPENCODE_SERVER_USERNAME`.

Without those env vars set, the server has no auth and is accessible directly on `localhost:4096`.

assistantResponseMessage.content:
Based on the plugin I built, here's how to interact with it:

**1. Add it to your config** (`.opencode/opencode.jsonc` or global config):

```json
{
  "plugin": ["openai-models-discovery"]
}
```

Or with options pre-filled (skips the GUI prompts):

```json
{
  "plugin": [
    ["openai-models-discovery", {
      "providerID": "ollama",
      "providerName": "Ollama",
      "baseURL": "http://localhost:11434/v1"
    }]
  ]
}
```

**2. Connect via the GUI:**

Run `opencode web` or the desktop app, then go to the provider connection flow (`/connect` in TUI). You'll see the new provider listed. The auth flow will prompt you for:
- Base URL (if not pre-configured in options)
- API Key

**3. Once connected**, the plugin automatically fetches `/v1/models` from your endpoint and populates all available models in the model selector.

Since it's registered as an internal plugin, it loads automatically on startup — no npm install needed. The provider appears in the model picker in both the web UI and TUI after authentication.

64: function buildModel(entry: OpenAIModelEntry, providerID: string, baseURL: string, opts: Options): Model {
65:   return {
66:     id: entry.id,
67:     providerID,
68:     api: {
69:       id: entry.id,
70:       url: baseURL,
71:       npm: "@ai-sdk/openai-compatible",
72:     },
73:     name: entry.id,
74:     capabilities: inferCapabilities(entry.id, opts.defaults),
75:     cost: {

88:   }
89: }

91: async function fetchModels(baseURL: string, apiKey: string): Promise<OpenAIModelEntry[]> {
92:   const url = `${baseURL.replace(/\/$/, "")}/models`
93:   log.info("fetching models", { url })
94: 
95:   const res = await fetch(url, {
96:     headers: { Authorization: `Bearer ${apiKey}` },
97:   }).catch((e) => {
98:     log.error("failed to fetch models", { error: e })
99:     return undefined
100:   })
101: 
102:   if (!res || !res.ok) {
103:     log.warn("models endpoint returned non-ok", { status: res?.status })
104:     return []
105:   }
106: 
107:   const body: OpenAIModelsResponse = await res.json()
108:   return body.data.filter((entry) => !entry.object || entry.object === "model")
109: }
110: 
111: export async function OpenAIModelsDiscoveryPlugin(_input: PluginInput, options?: PluginOptions): Promise<Hooks> {
112:   const opts = (options ?? {}) as Options
113:   const providerID = opts.providerID ?? "openai-compatible"
114:   const providerName = opts.providerName ?? "OpenAI Compatible"
115:   const baseURL = opts.baseURL ?? ""
116: 
117:   return {
118:     async config(config: Config) {
119:       if (!config.provider) config.provider = {}
120:       if (config.provider[providerID]) return
121: 
122:       config.provider[providerID] = {
123:         name: providerName,
124:         npm: "@ai-sdk/openai-compatible",
125:         ...(baseURL && { api: baseURL }),
126:         ...(opts.apiKeyEnv && { env: [opts.apiKeyEnv] }),
127:       }
128:     },
129:

131:       provider: providerID,
132:       methods: [

134:           type: "api",
135:           label: "API Key",
136:           prompts: [
137:             ...(baseURL
138:               ? []
139:               : [
